learn.title: How Electric Vehicles Work
learn.intro: Electric vehicles (EVs) are central to decarbonizing transport. By replacing internal combustion engines with efficient electric motors, we drastically reduce tailpipe emissions and reliance on fossil fuels. Understanding how they store, convert, and recycle energy is key to grasping the future of sustainable mobility.
learn.c1_text: Instead of burning fuel, an EV uses electricity from a battery to power an electric motor. Think of it like a high-tech fan: electricity creates a magnetic field that spins the motor, turning the wheels instantly without the lag of a traditional engine.
learn.c2_text: When you lift your foot off the accelerator, the motor runs in reverse, acting like a generator. This converts the car's kinetic energy back into electricity, sending it back to the battery. It is like catching water in a bucket while it flows downhill.
learn.c3_text: Charging varies from slow AC home chargers to rapid DC stations. In India, the FAME II scheme is accelerating the rollout of public charging stations to support the growing number of electric two-wheelers and cars on the road.
learn.stat1: EV efficiency is roughly 3-4x higher than gas cars
learn.stat2: Regenerative braking can extend range significantly
learn.stat3: India aims for 30% EV penetration by 2030
learn.stat4: DC fast chargers can charge 80% in 30 mins

learn.title: How Solar PV Generates Electricity
learn.intro: Solar Photovoltaics (PV) is one of the fastest-growing renewable technologies. By converting sunlight directly into electricity, we can tap into an inexhaustible energy source, reducing our dependence on carbon-intensive coal and gas power plants worldwide.
learn.c1_text: When sunlight hits a solar cell, it knocks electrons loose from their atoms. Think of it like a game of billiards: the incoming photon (the cue ball) hits an electron (the object ball), sending it flying and creating an electrical current.
learn.c2_text: Solar cells use layers of semiconductor material, like silicon, to create an internal electric field. This field acts like a one-way street, forcing the loose electrons to flow in a specific direction, creating the direct current (DC) we use.
learn.c3_text: India is a global solar leader, leveraging its tropical climate to drive massive capacity. The government's focus on rooftop solar and large-scale parks is transforming the national energy landscape and reducing import dependency.
learn.stat1: Solar capacity in India exceeds 70 GW
learn.stat3: Silicon is the most common PV material
learn.stat4: Solar costs have dropped by 80% since 2010

learn.title: India's Solar Manufacturing Incentives
learn.intro: To achieve energy independence, countries must manufacture their own renewable components. India's Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme is a strategic move to build a robust domestic solar supply chain, reducing reliance on imported silicon wafers and cells.
learn.c1_text: The PLI scheme provides financial incentives to manufacturers based on their incremental sales of high-efficiency solar modules. It is like a 'performance bonus' for companies that build large-scale factories within India, encouraging massive industrial growth.
learn.c2_text: By incentivizing local production, India aims to control the entire value chain—from polysilicon to finished modules. This ensures that the transition to green energy is not disrupted by global trade tensions or supply shortages.
learn.c3_text: The MNRE is driving domestic manufacturing to meet India's ambitious 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity target by 2030, ensuring the local economy benefits from the massive influx of green investment.
learn.stat2: Goal: Reduce dependence on imported cells

learn.title: Balancing the Electricity Grid
learn.intro: The power grid is a delicate balancing act. Because electricity is consumed the moment it is produced, grid operators must match supply with demand in real-time to prevent outages and ensure a stable, reliable energy flow.
learn.c1_text: Think of the grid like a water pipe system. If you turn the tap on too high without enough pressure, the pipes burst; if it's too low, nothing comes out. Operators must constantly adjust generation to match usage.
learn.c2_text: Renewables like wind and solar are variable. To manage this, grid operators use 'pinning reserves' (backup plants) and advanced forecasting to predict when clouds might block the sun or wind might die down, maintaining stability.
learn.c3_text: India is integrating massive amounts of solar and wind into its national grid. This requires advanced 'Green Energy Corridors' to transport renewable power from resource-rich states to high-demand industrial centers efficiently.
learn.stat1: Grid frequency must stay near 50/60 Hz

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology
learn.intro: Electric vehicles are more than just transport; they are mobile batteries. Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology allows EVs to give power back to the grid, turning millions of cars into a massive, distributed energy storage system.
learn.c1_text: Standard charging is one-way (grid to car). V2G uses bi-directional chargers, allowing electricity to flow both ways. It is like having a giant power bank that you can use to charge your phone or power your house.
learn.c2_text: When demand spikes (e.g., a hot summer afternoon), EVs can discharge a small amount of energy back into the grid. This reduces the need to turn on expensive, polluting 'peaker' gas plants, stabilizing the whole system.
learn.c3_text: V2G allows EV owners to participate in 'demand response' programs. In many markets, owners can earn money by selling their battery capacity back to the grid during peak times, effectively lowering the cost of owning an EV.
learn.stat4: Creates new revenue for EV owners

learn.title: Next-Gen Perovskite Solar Cells
learn.intro: Silicon has long dominated the solar market, but it is approaching its theoretical efficiency limit. Perovskite solar cells represent the next frontier, offering the potential for much higher efficiencies and much lower manufacturing costs.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites are a class of materials with a specific crystal structure. They are incredibly efficient at absorbing light. Unlike bulky silicon, perovskite layers can be made extremely thin, making them lightweight and flexible.
learn.c2_text: The most exciting application is 'tandem cells,' where a perovskite layer is placed on top of a standard silicon cell. The perovskite catches high-energy blue light, while the silicon catches lower-energy red light, maximizing total energy capture.
learn.c3_text: While promising, perovskites are sensitive to moisture and heat. Researchers are working to encapsulate these cells to ensure they last 25+ years, matching the lifespan required for commercial solar installations in harsh climates like India.
